Transcrição Completa: 15 Anatomy Of A Perfect Client

 

 

[00:00]"Hey guys, what's going on? So, when you're first getting started or when you're scaling up your agency, doesn't matter where you're at, you need to know what the perfect client is for you in your business."

 

[00:10]"And you need to know the people that you absolutely would never do business with. Okay? The perfect client traits and the traits of the do nots we'd never do business with this kind of person list is something that's going to constantly evolve as you grow."

 

[00:24]"Me being in my agency where I'm at, I've still to this very day, month by month, refined my list of what my perfect client traits are and what the list of traits are for people we would never do business with."

 

[00:37]"This comes extremely in handy when you're duplicating yourself and duplicating the beliefs and values of your organization into salespeople who are going to be responsible for bringing people into your business at a certain point of your agency."

 

[00:51]"And in addition to that, your actual operational staff and the people that you contract to. Everybody should know in your operational pipeline from salespeople to the staff to your accountants to any executives you might bring on."

 

[01:05]"Even if it's just yourself when you're first getting started, you need to know your perfect client traits so you know who to fire when and why."

 

[01:13] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Perfect Client Traits List"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Tela de smartphone sendo exibida, lista estruturada de características de clientes perfeitos, formato de documento organizado

 

[01:15]"Okay, you're seeing me hold my phone here. I have my perfect client traits list. I pulled this right from my actual list that I use inside of my staff. I'm going to read a word for word to you."

 

[01:25]"Some of them are going to be showing up here on the side, but if you guys want to in the Facebook group, you guys can actually go in, search perfect client trades, and you'll see this exact list there as well."

 

[01:35]"Which you can duplicate into your agency, make it specific for you, see if the shoe fits."

 

[01:41]"Now, to be very clear, you're likely going to learn the hard way, meaning through the experience of unfortunately taking on the wrong clients. You're going to learn who you do not want to do business with."

 

[01:54]"The best way to approach this exact lesson is when you are fielding for new clients, meaning you're generating leads and you're looking for people to close, looking for some new deals, you want to make sure that you always keep your perfect client traits in mind."

 

[02:09]"You do not want to time bomb. Meaning, you don't want to take on somebody that you even question is the wrong client. You want only perfect clients to be coming into your business."

 

[02:20]"That way, you have long relationships with all the people who you bring into your agency. You have long recurring revenue sources, great relationships, people that bring and mutually respect what you're doing in the deal together."

 

[02:33]"Meaning, when you choose to do business with somebody, you are mutually involved. It is an exchange on both sides."

 

[02:40]"Although they're paying money and expecting a result, you are also taking your time, energy, and intention and your staff's time, energy, and attention and putting it into the deal."

 

[02:51]"So anyway, without further ado, I'm just going to kind of read off on the list here what the do nots are that I've learned throughout my years running my agency and some things I've also learned by watching other agency owners how they fail and taking those and being like, you know what, I would never want that kind of person either."

 

[03:09] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "DO NOTS - Absolute Deal Breakers"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Lista de características negativas em formato de bullet points, cor vermelha ou indicativa de proibição, layout organizado verticalmente

 

[03:11]"So here's the do nots. Okay, the absolutes. We don't want them."

 

[03:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Don't Understand Marketing or Online Sales"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Ícone de confusão ou desconhecimento, representação de gap de conhecimento em marketing digital

 

[03:17]"Okay, they don't understand marketing or online sales. We don't want that kind of person. We don't want the person that we have to overeducate on very simple basic lessons."

 

[03:27]"We don't want the person who doesn't understand what content and direct response marketing do. We want people who are familiar with those vocabulary words, familiar with that vernacular and overall understand the process that we're going to be implementing for them."

 

[03:41]"Otherwise, we find in my agency throughout history that we have to spend too much time just on educating people on some of the most basic things and then when the things that matter need to be discussed that can make a real difference to the ROI that we're going to generate for the client, we can't have a real conversation with them and have them participate."

 

[03:59]"So, that's a do not for us. We do not want people who don't understand marketing or online sales."

 

[04:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Don't Pay What You Deserve or What's Fair"

 

NÚMEROS: $10K/month, $5K counteroffer (exemplo mencionado)

 

VISUAL: Comparação visual entre valor justo vs. valor oferecido, símbolo de desvalorização, balança desequilibrada

 

[04:07]"They don't pay you what you deserve or what's fair constantly, especially when you're first getting started. Your standards are pretty low because you're just looking to generate some cash flow and feel secure by proving that you can do this, right? That's the wrong way to do it."

 

[04:23]"You want to charge what you're worth right away and you want to in increase your prices through time as well. You want to constantly be re-evaluating and raising your standards."

 

[04:33]"But what I found through time is that there are absolutely going to be people when you say that your cost is 10k a month, they're going to say that they want to pay five."

 

[04:42]"And I found in my experience to never do deals with people like that because they're cheap. They're likely going to be bitching about the ad spend that they're going to have to put out as well just to get the result."

 

[04:54]"If you have to bring up any tools, softwares, resources, events that they could go to to become more educated, maybe they're likely going to have hesitancy towards it and not want to get involved or not want to do that or not want to invest because they're cheap or they don't value you."

 

[05:10]"That's even worse. If they look at you and they say, 'You know what? I just don't think you're worth what you're claiming you're worth.' That could be on your end needing to explain and articulate the value and what results you're going to be bringing to the person."

 

[05:24]"But let's be clear. If you feel that you've demonstrated value correctly, you've articulated the process to results, your CPR, clear process to revenue, and you brought true value to this person, you need to have a fair deal set up with them."

 

[05:38]"If they disrespect that, that's the wrong client in my eyes and in my agency eyes."

 

[05:43] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Complain a Lot and Micromanage You"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Ícone de pessoa reclamando, representação de microgerenciamento, clima de tensão e controle excessivo

 

[05:45]"Uh they complain a lot and micromanage you. We do not want people who are going to constantly be bitching about things."

 

[05:52]"Uh we need people to respect the process to get results. When I was first getting started and I would sell a lot of services that were very time and effort intensive such as building funnels, building marketing automation, building chat bots, etc."

 

[06:05]"These services will take a little bit of time and then they need leads and traffic sent to them for them to make money and to bring some revenue into the business, right?"

 

[06:14]"Well, in the process of creating those assets, I found that anybody that would complain about how long it took, number one, they didn't really understand the process."

 

[06:23]"And if I felt that we explained the process correctly, we used visual aids to demonstrate that. And then we still got to the point where they were micromanaging us, uh, being a little overbearing, if you will, and just overall not respecting the process, complaining about it. Wrong client."

 

[06:40]"We would fire them, make sure we refunded their money, and move on to take on the right clients because the wrong clients burn you down, take away your time, energy, and attention of you and your staff."

 

[06:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Do Not Give Reward-Based Incentives"

 

NÚMEROS: $10,000/month base, $20K bonus (exemplo)

 

VISUAL: Estrutura de compensação plana vs. estrutura com incentivos, gráfico de recompensas, modelo de performance-based payment

 

[06:54]"Okay, another one. They do not give you reward-based incentives. After I started studying through a recommendation of Mr. Tai Lopez and Charlie Munger, the 25 cognitive biases, there are a lot of different psychological benefits to exploiting different biases in your agreements that you create with different clients as an agency owner."

 

[07:15]"And one of the biggest ones that you can use in your favor are reward biases. If somebody doesn't understand what a reward what a reward bias is, it means that if they're going to pay you $10,000 a month to get them a result, if you say, 'Hey, client, if I make you this much, pay us 20K instead.'"

 

[07:34]"That's going to incentivize you as the agency owner and as the team handling the project to focus more on this client, to bring them more results, to make sure that your attention in a day goes towards that person rather than all the other things that you could do to bring in more money for yourself that have variable rewards."

 

[07:52]"When you have something that's just a flat fee or a flat service or a transactional service and there's no additional reward or incentive to continue doing it, there's not going to be an additional amount of time, energy, attention, and effort by you or your staff."

 

[08:05]"This also when you have a reward-based incentive opens you up for an opportunity to have with your staff reward-based incentives."

 

[08:13]"So rather than just you getting an additional kickback as the agency owner as the kingpin you need to also set up some rewards for your staff that way."

 

[08:22]"So a client that I found that doesn't respect the reward-based incentive means they might not understand the bias. They might not be to the level that we want them to be at as a client and that's not an ideal person that we'd want to do business with."

 

[08:36]"We want people who respect the reward-based incentives inside of my agency."

 

[08:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Disrespect Your Time and Your Team's Time"

 

NÚMEROS: 1:00 AM, 3:00 AM (horários de ligações inapropriadas), 3:00 PM agendado vs. 4:45/5:00 PM chegada real

 

VISUAL: Relógio indicando horários inapropriados, linha do tempo de desrespeito, calendário com compromissos não respeitados

 

[08:42]"Uh they disrespect your time and your team's time. So one time we had this client and they would constantly call myself and my team. They would never schedule a call."

 

[08:52]"You know, we would constantly tell them as well, hey, you know, we have no problem getting on the phone with you same day. We just need maybe an hour or two's notice so we can set aside the time to do it."

 

[09:03]"You know, we're constantly involved in all these different cycles. you're a priority to us, but just please give us a heads up and let us know that you need to be in in communication with us that day so we can schedule it in and respect everybody's time."

 

[09:17]"The client would still constantly just act like we didn't say that. Give us random calls throughout the day."

 

[09:23]"Uh the same client ends up getting tipsy, you know, they were a little they're a little bit drunk and it's 1:00 a.m. and they call my digital marketer and they were complaining about something and then uh 3:00 a.m. they call my social media marketer and they complain."

 

[09:37]"So, I had to fire the client the next morning, of course, because they disrespected my time and my team's time in several ways."

 

[09:45]"There's also people who have showed up to my office and they were supposed to meet me at 3, but you know, 4:45 or 5:00 rolls around and they're just showing up. That's the wrong client."

 

[09:56]"They disrespected my time. They think I just have an extra hour and a half, two hours laying around where I'm just I'm just chilling, just waiting for them. Absolutely not."

 

[10:05]"Always remember, your standards materialize into the life and business around you, and those standards start to matter."

 

[10:13]"So these do nots are very critical. Anybody who doesn't respect time or my team time, we don't work with."

 

[10:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Chaotic in Business and Personal Lives"

 

NÚMEROS: 9 meses de operação do negócio (exemplo do cliente)

 

VISUAL: Representação de caos: documentos desorganizados, problemas múltiplos, ambiente de trabalho desestruturado, cultura empresarial fraca

 

[10:22]"So they area chaotic. They are chaotic in their business and personal lives. One of my first clients ever, I abs I absolutely love the guy to be clear. There's there's nothing against this specific client that I'm referring to, but to be very direct through time, you know, I found that the client had a little bit of chaos going on in his life."

 

[10:42]"you know, he had uh a few different issues where the government would hit him with like regulation issues for for one of his brick-and-mortar businesses."

 

[10:51]"You know, he'd have some issues with his family here and there. You know, he'd um have different issues with relationships in his life for friends, family, business colleagues."

 

[11:01]"I always noticed his staff was always of a slightly lower caliber than what I had experienced in all these other clients that I was working with."

 

[11:09]"And I started to recognize, this is when I first got started, that the chaos in somebody's personal life can automatically leak in to their business because you are a representation as a personal individual of the business itself."

 

[11:23]"So that starts to duplicate. Your culture starts to duplicate into the business's culture."

 

[11:29]"So here's what happens. Okay, this client's personal chaos, you know, the guy's coming over to my house. We're doing things like webinars and just building out funnels and all this cool stuff."

 

[11:39]"I mean, all of a sudden, like my home whenever the guy would come over would just kind of get messed up, you know, like maybe maybe something would break in the kitchen, like a pan would would just like fall apart, you know, or uh one time out on my balcony here in Miami, I I have these nice wicker chairs and I come out the next morning and like one of the pads for the wicker chairs, which you sit on, was just completely gone, you know?"

 

[12:04]"I was like, where the hell that go? there. Everything after the person would leave would just would not be symmetrical, you know, like maybe a couch would be like offset or a rug would be messed up or something or uh some decorations in the home would be slightly altered."

 

[12:18]"But my point is personal chaos and business chaos are not something that I wanted to expose me or my business to because I have high standards."

 

[12:28]"And once again, whenever you lower your standards, that lowers the bar for you, your staff, and everything automatically. Your standards materialize into into your life and business around you."

 

[12:39]"So, long story short, we don't deal with people in my agency who are chaotic in their business or personal lives."

 

[12:46] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Constantly Complaining About Anything"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Metáfora do "garbage truck" (caminhão de lixo), representação de negatividade sendo espalhada, ambiente tóxico

 

[12:48]"Uh, they're constantly complaining about anything. So, besides just complaining about the process, anybody who complains that, as my old boss Grant Cardone would say, that person's a garbage truck."

 

[13:00]"Anybody who's bringing around negativity and just complaining, they're they're literally a garbage truck. They're just going around and just filth. Just filthy, you know, picking up filth, handing out filth, talking about gossip, all of that."

 

[13:14]"We don't want anything to do with it inside of my agency. I don't want my staff having to deal with it. We're very cautious about the data that gets fed into our heads. Okay?"

 

[13:24]"So, all our senses, we need the right data feeding into it constantly. The wrong data start feeding into your senses, you're going to start seeing the wrong results materialize in your life and business."

 

[13:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Don't Want to Spend More on Profitable Marketing"

 

NÚMEROS: $50,000 ad spend → $60,000 retorno (não lucrativo), $50,000 ad spend → $250,000-$500,000 retorno (lucrativo), proposta de escalar para $100,000-$150,000

 

VISUAL: Comparação ROI: cenário de baixo retorno vs. alto retorno, gráfico de escalabilidade, análise de lucratividade

 

[13:37]"Um, they don't want to spend more on profitable marketing. So, let's be clear. Sometimes marketers complain when the client doesn't want to increase their ad spend, but they're really not making the person a substantial amount of money on the ad spend they're already getting. Okay?"

 

[13:53]"So, that means if I have a client who is spending $50,000 and we're only making them back 60,000, you know, it's tough for me to go to the client and say, 'Hey, let's spend $75,000 this month because that doesn't make any sense, right? It wasn't that much profitable marketing.'"

 

[14:10]"However, when I have a client, they're spending $50,000. Let's say we're making them $250,000 or $500,000 off that $50,000 spent."

 

[14:19]"In that situation, I would want to go to our client and say, 'Hey, I want you to spend $100,000 this month or $150,000 this month because we have a profitable, proven marketing campaign that we want to scale up to get more results with.'"

 

[14:34]"If we have a client that's hesitant to that, we might not want to be working with that person because they might be taking all those profits and reinvesting it back into areas that don't impact and scale their business, which we don't want any part of."

 

[14:47]"We constantly want growth. We don't want to be dealing with people that might have some shaky financial situations and they're using all the money they make to cover those things rather than reinvesting their profits back into the business to make more profits."

 

[15:01] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Think Small"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Contraste entre mentalidade pequena vs. grande, representação de pensamento limitado, zona de conforto vs. expansão

 

[15:03]"So, they think small. It's a big do not for us. You know, we are big thinkers inside of my agency."

 

[15:10]"Uh in my business, and this is not to brag, this is to be clear. I didn't start this way. This is something we worked up to by continuing to increase our standards."

 

[15:18]"We do not want to work with people who think small because they take small actions. uh they can't comprehend big ideas and they'll shrink you, your staff and your thinking, which is even worse."

 

[15:31]"You know, I constantly in my agency and in my personal life want to make sure that anybody we're talking to is expanding our thinking and expanding their life and businesses."

 

[15:41]"Anybody who's thinking small is is potentially in a state of contraction. And if they're in contraction and they start associating with you, you're going to go into contraction as well."

 

[15:52]"You don't want all your staff to be conditioned to be thinking small because that'll take you away from working with the bigger projects that are out there that require bigger thinking to get."

 

[16:03] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Emotional and Annoying"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Representação de instabilidade emocional, falta de controle em situações de pressão, colapso sob stress

 

[16:05]"Okay, another one. They are emotional and annoying. I've actually had a few clients like this where they're they seem like very stable people. You know, they seem like very great entrepreneurs, well-rounded, and then we get into a slightly stressful situation."

 

[16:20]"Maybe a deadline's approaching and there's more communication that's required between us and the client. Really, things that aren't even that stressful. just approaching a deadline, all of a sudden the person will emotionally collapse or their ability to control themselves and have just a regulated conversation with you without getting emotional or annoying."

 

[16:40]"Uh, we don't like to work with people like that. You know, that's a big do not for us. Pretty pretty simple there."

 

[16:46] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Do Not Understand Your Results (After Clear Articulation)"

 

NÚMEROS: 9 meses de operação inicial, 5-6 meses de trabalho conjunto, 10% → 40% → 85% dos leads vindos do Facebook (vs. 15% YouTube)

 

VISUAL: Caso de estudo: transição de fonte de leads, gráfico de evolução percentual, comparação YouTube vs. Facebook/Instagram, conceito de dissonância cognitiva

 

[16:48]"They do not understand your results, you get them. And then I put in parentheses here after you've articulated visually with words in several ways."

 

[16:57]"So, I had this client uh he was making a lot of money on YouTube. Okay? He would have one video that was organically ranked in YouTube pretty high and before we started working with him that was a majority of his lead generation and a majority of where his revenue would come from from this one video that was ranked pretty high in search engines."

 

[17:17]"So his business was around for about 9 months and then we came in and we said hey it'd be a great idea for you to start doing Facebook ads. There's lead generation you can do on there. There's lead forms, instant forms, all kinds of cool stuff we could send people to your website."

 

[17:32]"we think that we could get more leads for your closers and then that would bring you a lot more revenue. So, of course, the person was like, 'Yeah, that sounds pretty good. You know, I'm making pretty good money now this way. I'd love to expand in that.'"

 

[17:44]"Well, here's where the issue came. This client was with us for about five, six months or so. And every single week when we would do our reporting, we would get more results than the week before."

 

[17:55]"So, when we first got started, keep in mind, everything was YouTube based, okay? All the lead generation, all the revenue."

 

[18:02]"Well, as soon as we came in, we increased the amount of leads that came to his business. You know, all of a sudden, 10% of his lead generation was from Facebook. Then a week later, 40% of all his leads that would come in would come from Facebook. And and a few weeks later, it counted up to 85%."

 

[18:20]"So 85% of this guy's leads started coming from Facebook and Instagram. 15% were coming from this YouTube video comparatively. Okay."

 

[18:29]"Cognitive dissence is where you operate off of old anchored data and you don't replace it with new data when new data is available and overrides the old data that you're operating on."

 

[18:42]"It is a is a bitch to get around somebody's cognitive dissonance. And what I learned the hard way, you don't want to be responsible for that. You're not a therapist, okay?"

 

[18:52]"But you need to know how therapists think so you can recognize these situations and in a healthy way get out by firing the client."

 

[19:00]"So every week when I would do my reporting, I would say, 'Hey, you know, we got you this much money this week. This is how many leads we brought in. This is the percentage of them that came off Facebook and Instagram, and this is the percentage that came off YouTube this week.'"

 

[19:15]"You know, we're seeing a substantial growth. We talk about percentages. We would never use our emotional interpretation. We would never use feelings to describe what's going on. Of course, we use data. Okay?"

 

[19:27]"So that means we explain things with the analytics from Facebook. We explain things from his CRM. We show him in the transactions that are coming in and what his salespeople are saying, where the leads are coming from, that they're coming from Facebook and Instagram."

 

[19:41]"Yet, every conversation, the guy would tell me and try to convince me that they were still coming from YouTube."

 

[19:48]"So, statistically, and looking at data and operating on data, 85% of this guy's business comes from our actions. Yet, in his head, he's literally trying to convince me every single week that they're still only coming from YouTube."

 

[20:03]"It was crazy, too. He would conversate with me via text and say things like, 'Oh, I'm having one of my biggest months ever. You know, just closed a deal off Facebook. You know, the guys are loving it.'"

 

[20:14]"And then every week, no matter what, when it came time to just simply accept the data for what it was, and talk about what we could do next with it, the client would reject it, operate on cognitive dissonance, which is taking old data and choosing to use that rather than the new data that's right in front of you in factual."

 

[20:34]"It was a simple choice the guy made. So, unfortunately, that created a big conflict. you know, the guy ended up charging back three of his payments."

 

[20:42]"We ended up, of course, winning because we had testimonials from the guy and all kinds of other cool stuff. We were in the right. Absolutely."

 

[20:49]"But to be clear, that's a situation where that's the wrong client. And if I would have recognized in that first month, oh wow, this guy's got cognitive dissonance. Every time I talk to him, he's not using the new data. I should have fired him and let him go. But instead, I had to learn the hard way."

 

[21:06]"So, long story short, we we want people who understand the results we're getting for him. We do not want people who do not understand the results."

 

[21:14] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Treat Others Around Them Poorly"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Representação de desrespeito: interações com staff, garçons, recepcionistas, ambiente de trabalho tóxico

 

[21:16]"Another do not. They treat others around them poorly. We do not want people who are disrespecting others around them, their staff, our staff. Uh even just a waiter or waitress if we're out, you know, if they're checking into a hotel and they're slightly mean. We don't like people like that. We're kind people."

 

[21:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Not Willing to Put in Work to Be Successful"

 

NÚMEROS: 15 minutos (duração de vídeo educacional mencionado)

 

VISUAL: Representação de preguiça vs. disciplina, livros não lidos, tarefas não completadas, falta de comprometimento

 

[21:37]"Another do not. They are not willing to put in the work to be successful. I've had so many clients where I'd tell them to go read a particular book or go go watch a 15inute video where they could be educated in the area where we were to further understand it so they could have a real conversation with us where we could potentially further the results and scale up just from the other person knowing what we know."

 

[22:01]"People wouldn't be willing to put in the work. Those were unsuccessful clients compared to the clients that are constantly putting in the work to be successful, putting in the time, putting in the discipline."

 

[22:11]"I want to be clear, guys. There's a lot of businesses who need our help, but there's also a lot of lazy ass business owners in the world who are not willing to put in the work on themselves, not willing to grow themselves, and just flat out not willing to really scale their business and not really uh being willing to address that personal growth as a requirement of business growth."

 

[22:32]"So, to be clear, we found that that was a big do not for us."

 

[22:36] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Think Marketers Are Miracle Workers"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Expectativa irreal: pílula mágica, trabalho zero do cliente, mentalidade passiva vs. parceria ativa

 

[22:38]"Uh, another do not, they think marketers are miracle workers and make it so they never have to do work again."

 

[22:45]"Uh there's been maybe one or two clients in my past over over the last three and a half years or so where people would say, you know, hey, I'm going to give you this money and they'll act like they're going to do all this stuff like we need clients to make ads."

 

[22:58]"You know, we need clients to give us photos and videos and just flat out take what we say and use it to help us get them more results."

 

[23:06]"Clients who don't think like that, you know, clients who once again not willing to put in the work and just think that marketers are the are the magic pill. Those are not the people we want to be working with."

 

[23:17]"It requires a mutual exchange when we go into a business with somebody. That way we can get the most results for them."

 

[23:24] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Try to Pay in Equity Only (No Service Fee)"

 

NÚMEROS: 5,000+ entrevistas na plataforma, 2.5 meses de trabalho não pago, centenas de milhões de dólares (net worth do sócio), 3 chargebacks

 

VISUAL: Caso de estudo de equity deal fracassado, plataforma com bilionários entrevistados, Mark Cuban/Broadcast.com mencionados, funil 95% completo não lançado

 

[23:26]"Another do not. There's only a few more do nots here. They try to pay you in equity when they have not made any money yet and are not willing to pay you a service fee."

 

[23:36]"You guys will hear this more than anything else, especially when you're first getting started. You're going to have a lot of people just straight up try to take advantage of you."

 

[23:44]"They'll look at you and say, 'Oh, wow. I have this new business.' You know, they'll make it sound all exciting, and I would love for you to come in. I'll give you a chunk of equity. you know, we haven't made any money yet."

 

[23:56]"Even if they've made a little money, you know, why aren't you willing to pay me a service fee plus equity? I've got contractors I got to pay. I'm not asking you for a service fee just to ask for it. I'm asking for it so my business can make a profit."

 

[24:10]"We're a for-profit company. You know, we don't want to put in a lot of work upfront and not get paid for it along the way. We want both short-term and long-term rewards."

 

[24:20]"I had this company, I'm not I'm not going to say the name of it because they still operate today, but big company, over 5,000 interviews done on their platform. uh billionaires were interviewed on the platform, right?"

 

[24:33]"And there's this guy, he sold a company to Mark Cuban and then he got a piece of Mark Cuban's Broadcast.com company that he built and sold. And this is a really high trustworthy guy. The guy's net worth was hundreds of millions of dollars, right?"

 

[24:47]"I had gone into the deal with two of my mentors at the time. They brought me in because they needed somebody who was good at funnels and they needed an agency that was good with running traffic and just overall managing the creative process to build out this information product the company wanted to make."

 

[25:05]"So anyway, they have these 5,000 interviews. You know, they're building a payw wall for it, which means they're getting ready to sell all that content rather than just give it away for free."

 

[25:15]"And we're we we built a whole funnel. It took us two and a half months. They didn't pay us. Uh they said that they were going to give us equity the whole time."

 

[25:24]"This is another big red flag. They didn't give us a signed agreement upfront for the equity that they claimed they were going to pay us."

 

[25:32]"Still to this day, that company has not generated a dollar in cash flow because they have an unfinished funnel because they chose not to pay us and not give us equity, not be contractually um on the same page, if that makes sense."

 

[25:47]"And at the at the five yard line, meaning at the very last uh set of actions that needed to be taken to launch the funnel, you know, they didn't want to sign an agreement with us to give us the equity or to pay us anything."

 

[26:00]"So, in total, that was a big loss. I mean, two and a half months worth of opportunity cost, staff time, not taking on other projects. I was aggressively building out my personality brand at that same time."

 

[26:12]"Just once again, equity deals where you're not getting paid. Even in situations where there's somebody of extremely high credibility, get it in paper, guys."

 

[26:22]"Don't take on equity deals without getting a service fee. I'm telling you, it's not worth it."

 

[26:27] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Eat Poorly (Causes Poor Cognitive Function)"

 

NÚMEROS: 40% redução no fluxo sanguíneo cerebral (efeito do café), espresso duplo a cada 10 minutos

 

VISUAL: Exemplo de almoço em NY: homem com sobrepeso, múltiplos espressos duplos, comida não saudável, MRI mostrando redução de fluxo sanguíneo cerebral

 

[26:29]"Um, okay, last one here. So, they eat poorly. This causes poor cognitive functionality. I'm not projecting a particular diet on you, although I'm vegan."

 

[26:40]"Uh there are different people out there who just eat like shit You know, I was at a I was at a lunchon one time with this potential client up in New York and a big heavy set guy and the entire time and I mean this every 10 minutes the guy would order another double espresso and I was like what the hell he's eating French fries and like pork sandwiches and every 10 minutes you know another shot of espresso"

 

[27:06]"and what I understand about coffee it lowers your brain flow by 40%. You know, there's videos you can go look up where people are under an MRI machine and literally they drink coffee, boom, blood flow 40% reduced."

 

[27:19]"And I'm over here like, wow, you know, this can't be healthy. This guy's probably not even understanding what I'm saying right now. You know, he's got a 40% lower probability of understanding it anyway. Right?"

 

[27:30]"So, long story short, those are my do nots. Okay? We do not do business with anybody like that."

 

[27:36]"And as I said, that constantly evolves, constantly changes, just evolves through experience. And at this point in my life, uh, seeing what others are also doing that are are big red flags for us that we do not do that will be added to this list."

 

[27:51] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "PERFECT CLIENT TRAITS - The DOs"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Lista positiva em verde ou cor indicativa de aprovação, características ideais organizadas, contraste com lista de "do nots"

 

[27:53]"I'm going kind of go faster through these other ones. These are the dos. Okay, so these are the perfect client traits. So in this list, this is who we want to work with and why we want to work with them."

 

[28:04] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Respects Our Work"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Representação de respeito mútuo, reconhecimento de valor, apreciação profissional

 

[28:06]"So number one, respects our work. The perfect client respects our work and what it takes to produce massive results for them. Simple respect. We want it"

 

[28:17] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Understands Our Work"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Cliente educado em marketing, familiaridade com terminologia: funnels, automation, chatbots, email broadcasts, Zapier, payment integration, sales follow-up

 

[28:19]"uh we want people to understand what we do. understands our work. Okay, so we've worked with a lot of businesses in the past that have not understood anything about marketing, advertising, funnels, marketing automation, chat bots, email broadcast, zap year, payment integration, sales, follow-up, etc."

 

[28:38]"When clients don't understand what we do, uh they can't understand how to maximize what we're doing for them without us holding their hand."

 

[28:46]"And as I said, we don't want to be holding people's hands the entire way. We want somewhat educated people that we can empower further with more knowledge, but we want them to be willing to do the work."

 

[28:56]"We want clients who are willing to put in the work, understand what we can do, so they can bring profitable ideas to the table and so they can participate in strategy conversations with us as it's their business anyway, right? So anyway,"

 

[29:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Cares About Results, Not Time and Effort"

 

NÚMEROS: 3 horas/semana de trabalho (exemplo hipotético)

 

VISUAL: Foco em ROI vs. horas trabalhadas, mentalidade orientada a resultados, valor entregue vs. tempo gasto

 

[29:10]"uh cares about results, not time and effort. Anybody who we generate leads, revenue for, it's a positive ROI, we're doing our job correctly, we're constantly scaling their business."

 

[29:22]"I I do not want us a person to say, 'Well, you only worked three hours this week. You know, that's not enough. I want you to work much more.' That is not what it's about."

 

[29:32]"We don't want the client who's scrutinizing our time and effort. When we are in business, we are in business to generate people results."

 

[29:40]"If it takes us an hour a month, we don't want any bitching about what it took to get the result. We just want to focus on the results with the clients that we choose to work with."

 

[29:50]"So, long story short, we just want people who care about results."

 

[29:53] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Has a Small Team Already"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Estrutura de equipe pequena mas funcional, posições criativas existentes, capacidade de gestão demonstrada

 

[29:55]"Okay, has a small team already. What I found in my business, the people who are just getting started, especially the people bootstrapping in business or they just flat out don't have the right skills, a team, they're not going to know how to manage a team."

 

[30:10]"They're not going to know how to keep you accountable. And accountability is necessary in success from both parties."

 

[30:16]"In addition to that, we like to leverage the skill sets that are already in somebody's internal team that they might have."

 

[30:23]"So, we love people who already have different skills, especially creative positions. That way we can go in and essentially consult with those people, make them even more profitable in the positions they already have hired and really maximize what's already going on to scale up results."

 

[30:40]"So having a small team means more probability for us to make them more money than what we would if it was just our team and a singular business owner as an example."

 

[30:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Already Has Assets to Work With"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Infraestrutura existente: funnels, sistemas, processos prontos para otimização vs. construção do zero

 

[30:52]"We want people who already have the assets to work with us. We do not want to work with people with shitty funnels or no funnels at all."

 

[31:00]"Uh, of course I know there's some of you here that will build funnels and that's great, but you're selling time and effort when you sell a funnel."

 

[31:07]"We want to work with people who we can come in. We're rain makers. We want to drive more leads, drive more sales, make more money."

 

[31:15]"We want to get paid incentivized result rewards whenever we make people more money past what we projected."

 

[31:22]"We do not want to be doing all the grunt work because what I found in my agency, I sold all that stuff, guys. You know, I sold all the time and effort intensive tasks and services that you could sell as an agency owner."

 

[31:34]"And all of those were disrespected compared to just getting people results that takes little bits of time. People love it when you're making more money driving more leads."

 

[31:44]"They disrespect the hell out of the process that it takes to get to that point, right?"

 

[31:49]"So, if somebody's not positioned already to make money and to bring leads in online, we found that the process of making funnels, if we're the person making their first funnel, they don't even have an idea of what to expect."

 

[32:02]"They don't have an idea of how long it takes. They might just think that, oh, I have a funnel now. That means I'm automatically going to make money."

 

[32:09]"They might not understand you got to send traffic to it and that you got to tweak the process. They might not understand the variables that get the results."

 

[32:17]"So, my point is, when you're choosing to do time and effort intensive services, you're essentially putting yourself in the liability to be disrespected by somebody not respecting that process of what it takes just to be positioned to start getting results. Right?"

 

[32:33]"We're not in the game of positioning people to get results. We're in the game of getting people more results. Does that make sense? That's where the most money is anyway."

 

[32:42]"So anyway, long story short, already has assets needed to work with us. We're not in the game of making that. Okay?"

 

[32:49] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "We're Rainmakers, Not Handymen"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Contraste: tarefas de baixo valor (handyman) vs. ações de alta receita (rainmaker), hierarquia de valor no trabalho

 

[32:51]"We're rain makers, not handymen. We handymen are paid for tasks. Okay? There are small and usually insignificant."

 

[33:00]"Sure, the handyman is needed for the grand scheme of success. However, we are not handymen in my agency. We are rain makers."

 

[33:08]"We get paid to get clients results and get paid well to do so. We take the highest revenue-driven actions and that's it. Nothing else."

 

[33:17]"We can come into a business. If the highest revenue driven action is building a chatbot, we'll build a chatbot. If the highest revenue driven action is some direct response ads on Facebook or Instagram, that's what we'll do."

 

[33:29]"Whatever it is that's necessary to take the highest revenue driven actions, that's what we want to provide as agency owners, not the menial tasks that we want to leave for the handyman."

 

[33:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Have Deep Pockets"

 

NÚMEROS: $10,000-$20,000/mês para contractors de serviço

 

VISUAL: Capacidade financeira robusta, investimento contínuo, orçamento para crescimento e escala

 

[33:42]"Uh we want people to have deep pockets. So constantly in business, I found as an agency owner, when I'm taking on clients that flat out don't have a lot of money. They don't have deep pockets."

 

[33:54]"They are the people who are cheap. They're the people who don't invest heavily into their business, they're usually the people as well that take money that's made from the business and invest it back into their personal life or hoard the money by saving it instead of using it to expand and to grow and to scale."

 

[34:11]"We don't want people who are who are u shallow pocketed, right? We want people with lots of money because it's going to take money to work with us."

 

[34:20]"It's going to take money to advertise. It's going to take money to build results and outsource to other contractors they got to work with. It's going to take money to have production teams behind the scenes, right?"

 

[34:30]"Long story short, we want clients who are comfortable with paying things at 10,000, 20,000 a month just for service contractors that are going to produce them results because that's what we're in the game to do."

 

[34:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Offer Reward-Based Incentives"

 

NÚMEROS: Dan Lok exemplo: $25K base → $75K/mês ($150K/dia produzido) → $150K/mês ($300K/dia produzido), crescimento de centenas de milhares para milhões por mês

 

VISUAL: Estrutura escalonada de compensação, caso de sucesso Dan Lok, modelo de incentivos progressivos

 

[34:44]"Uh we want clients who offer reward-based incentives. We've talked a lot about that, but as another example, we want somebody like our guy Dan Lock, right?"

 

[34:53]"Dan Lock, one of our clients, we've helped him significantly scale up his business from a few hundred thousand dollar a month to a few million dollars a month. And it's been a hell of a ride and it's going to continue scaling."

 

[35:05]"But somebody like Dan, he originally paid us 25K to be hands-on in his business. Through time, he evolved to say, 'Jeremy, if you make me 150k a day, I'll pay you 75k a month instead. If you make me 300k a day, I'll pay you 150k a month instead.'"

 

[35:24]"That is a hell of an incentive for us to work much harder, much faster, and keep much more of our focus internally in my agency on that specific client."

 

[35:33]"You want people who are going to do the same, respect you and give you reward-based incentives,"

 

[35:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "View Us as Experts"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Posicionamento de autoridade, confiança profissional, respeito por expertise, autonomia para executar

 

[35:40]"they view us as an expert as we are. So, we know what we know and we know it well. And when we go into a business and we tell people, hey, here's what we recommend. Here's what we think you should do. Here's the highest revenue driven actions. Let's just go ahead and knock them out."

 

[35:56]"We don't want resistance. We do want thoughtful agreement or disagreement that can help further the process to getting results, but we do not want people who are going to say, you know, that's that's not what we feel like we should do."

 

[36:10]"You hired us as a consultant or as an agency owner and that's what we think we should do and we're going to do it unless you have a logical uh approach and can build on top of what we said because this is what you hired us to do."

 

[36:24]"We want people once again who view us as the experts we are. Because when you have that expert positioning, you have the authority to do what's necessary. If the trust is high, the process to getting results and the speed to getting results is high."

 

[36:39] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Take Smart Actions Quickly"

 

NÚMEROS: 30 dias (ciclo de pagamento)

 

VISUAL: Velocidade de execução, decisões rápidas, maximização mensal, momentum

 

[36:41]"Uh they take smart actions quickly. When we present a set of actions to take, once again, we don't want resistance. We don't want time in between the actions. We want to knock these things out, especially because we're getting paid every 30 days."

 

[36:56]"We want to maximize every single month that we work with somebody. So, we can get paid more the next month by having somebody who's willing to take smart actions quickly."

 

[37:06]"Speed is a requirement to get results faster, especially when you're doing something that's result driven."

 

[37:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Operate on Data, Not Opinion"

 

NÚMEROS: 85% Facebook/Instagram vs. 15% YouTube (referência ao caso anterior)

 

VISUAL: Analytics, métricas, decisões baseadas em dados vs. sentimentos, evitar dissonância cognitiva

 

[37:14]"We want people to operate on data, not opinion. I gave you guys that story a little bit ago of the client who had cognitive dissence, kept trying to convince me all his leads and sales were coming from YouTube when in reality they were coming from Facebook and Instagram."

 

[37:30]"That is an example of somebody who's operating on opinion and not data. We do not want that. It creates a lot of friction and it obviously creates a lot of problems."

 

[37:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Willing to Invest in Necessary Tools, Resources, Coaching, Mentoring"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Investimento contínuo em desenvolvimento: livros, eventos, coaches, ferramentas, educação da equipe

 

[37:42]"We want people who are willing to invest in the necessary tools, resources, coaching, and mentoring."

 

[37:48]"As I've described a few times here, we want to make sure that the clients we work with are willing to invest into their team, are willing to invest into us to further educate us."

 

[37:58]"We want to make sure that they're educating themselves, buying books, going to events, hiring coaches."

 

[38:04]"We love people who are constantly involved in personal development and personal progress because that is business development, business progress."

 

[38:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Hold Us Accountable to Perform"

 

NÚMEROS: Relatórios diários ou semanais

 

VISUAL: Sistema de accountability: calls regulares, reporting estruturado, monitoramento de resultados, transparência

 

[38:14]"We want people to hold us accountable to perform. I love it when clients want to call me, call my team, and ask, 'Hey, how much are you making us right now? What are the leads that you guys are producing us right now? you know, can we have a can we have a daily report sent to us or a weekly report sent to us?"

 

[38:32]"Because then my team and myself are held accountable to get more results."

 

[38:37]"When you work with a client who talks to you only a few times throughout the month, they don't even ask you about the results that you're generating them. That's going to be somebody who has a lower probability to renew the agreements with you in the following month and continue paying you."

 

[38:51]"People who aren't necessarily focused on the result, they're not going to be worried about the results that you're getting them when they come."

 

[38:58]"If they keep their eye on the prize, you know that they're going to be focused on expanding your relationship more, maximizing the value that you guys have, and you're going to find yourself being a little more involved in the process, which can get you more fulfillment, more happiness, and just overall get your team more involved and skilled up."

 

[39:16] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Respect Our Time"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Calls agendadas vs. interrupções aleatórias, respeito por horários, comunicação estruturada

 

[39:18]"Uh, we want people who respect our time. As I said, we do not want people who hire us and then are constantly calling us without scheduling calls."

 

[39:27]"We don't want people who are reactive in their businesses and just taking all of our time away from us because people aren't paying us for time and effort. People are paying us for results."

 

[39:36]"So, the the point I'm making is schedule out conversations, not random conversations that happen randomly or texts where they sporadically need you all of a sudden or, you know, those awful calls where it happens extremely late at night and they're complaining about something."

 

[39:52]"You don't want any of that, okay? You want somebody who respects your time."

 

[39:56] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Smart Clients Only"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Inteligência, capacidade de compreensão, comunicação efetiva, alinhamento intelectual

 

[39:58]"We want smart clients only. So, I've said this before. We want people not just taking smart actions, but who are actually smart. Okay?"

 

[40:07]"You don't want somebody who you're going to have an intellectual problem with because that's a communication problem. Usually,"

 

[40:14] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Like Money More Than Being Right"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Ego vs. Lucro, humildade para aceitar melhores estratégias, flexibilidade mental, foco em resultados

 

[40:16]"uh, they like money more than being right. Constantly, people will have an ego problem in business where they will need to be right more than they'll need to be positioned to make money."

 

[40:28]"And that is a very limiting factor that somebody can have with you in a business relationship."

 

[40:34]"So, let's say that you're conversating about something that you're going to do or that you could do or that you did to get results. and they might say, 'You know what? That's not the way to do it. I want to do it this way.'"

 

[40:46]"Whether all the data and every piece of supporting evidence and case study and just, you know, even the even the exact steps of how to do it are right in front of them."

 

[40:56]"You want somebody who's going to look at you and say, 'You know what? That's probably going to make more money than what I'm saying. Let's not do what I'm saying. Let's do that instead.'"

 

[41:05]"Versus the person's ego just needing to be right in that example. I faced that in a few different clients. Definitely don't want that."

 

[41:12]"So, we want people who like money more than being right."

 

[41:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Willing to Invest Into Our Success"

 

NÚMEROS: $50,000 mastermind (exemplo), 10 business owners (tamanho do grupo)

 

VISUAL: Oportunidades compartilhadas: eventos, masterminds (Tony Robbins, Keith Cunningham, Funnel Hacker Live), treinamentos pagos, investimento mútuo

 

[41:17]"We also want clients that are willing to invest into our success. So, I have clients all the time, you know, they're going to a event or they're going to a Tony Robbins mastermind or there's a $50,000 mastermind with like 10 business owners there that are all high level."

 

[41:34]"I constantly get all these opportunities from my clients. Hey, we're going to this Keith Cunningham event. We're going to Funnel Hacker Live, you know, where let let us pay for your hotel. Let us pay for your coaching."

 

[41:45]"Hey, we got this guy with this company culture. you know, we're gonna have him talk to you and your team."

 

[41:50]"We want people who invest back into our success because that increases our ability to get them more success. You see what I mean?"

 

[41:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Put in Work Required to Be Successful"

 

NÚMEROS: Ano 1: $550,000 em receita, Anos 2-3: $3+ milhões (após educação em marketing)

 

VISUAL: Caso de sucesso Lee Haight/Sky Diamonds University, crescimento exponencial através de auto-educação, Russell Brunson's Inner Circle

 

[42:00]"Uh they put in the work required to be successful. So, to be very clear, there was one client, my very first client ever, his name's Lee Hate. He's a great guy. I love Lee."

 

[42:10]"The first year of me helping build, it was really just me and Lee. I was his marketer and Lee was the the sales guy and you know, the personality brand."

 

[42:19]"And we created this company called Sky Diamonds University which helped roofers and contractors. It's what it's expanded into now with running their business, helped train their salespeople specific to roofing and door knockocking, etc."

 

[42:33]"And we did about $550,000 in that first year together. But we could have done much more if Lee would have educated himself more in the area of marketing. Let me explain."

 

[42:45]"Over a year later, so now we're going into year two to three, Lee's done over $3 million that year because he chose to educate himself on marketing."

 

[42:55]"So when Lee can have more conversations with me, he invested in the Russell's inner circle. He's read all kinds of these books be he's one of the people as an entrepreneur that I can exchange more book ideas to like he this is a good one, that's a good one, try this."

 

[43:11]"And because he's constantly feeding himself that kind of data, we find that that one variable has helped made him more successful."

 

[43:19]"It's increased our rapport together and overall it's just made everything more smooth because there's more understanding. more understanding, more awareness equals an easier um more calculated, manageable life in business."

 

[43:33]"Okay, so that's what we enjoy specifically with Lee as an example."

 

[43:37] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Already Making Lots of Money, Want to Make More"

 

NÚMEROS: $1M atual → $10M objetivo (1 ano) → $100M visão (2 anos), $3M/mês → $4M/mês (exemplo de crescimento)

 

VISUAL: Trajetória de crescimento exponencial, ambição escalável, mentalidade de expansão contínua

 

[43:39]"So, they're already making lots of money and want to make even more. People who say, you know, I'm making three million a month. I don't know if I want to make four. We don't want to work with that kind of person."

 

[43:51]"We want the person who says, listen, I'm doing a million dollars right now. I want to do 10 million next year, and about two years later, I want to be at a hundred million. Can you help me exponentially grow my business?"

 

[44:04]"and is that real? Show me how we could do it with math."

 

[44:07]"We love clients like that that are data driven and more importantly that want to exponentially expand because that's a lot of opportunity there, a lot of reinvestment and more importantly a lot of experience that you can accumulate as an agency owner."

 

[44:21] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Have Good Team, Want to Make Them Great"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Cultura de crescimento interno, investimento em desenvolvimento de equipe, transformação de bom para excelente

 

[44:23]"Okay, one of the last ones here, they have a good team that they want to invest into making great."

 

[44:29]"We love, as I said, people who already have a small team, but we love the entrepreneur who says, 'You know what? These people that I have working for me. They're already doing good, but I know that they can all be better with more more training, more skill, more awareness."

 

[44:44]"We would love for you to come in and help my staff go from good to great because that means they have an internal culture of growth. So, that's a great perfect client trait for us. Uh, we thoroughly enjoy it.'"

 

[44:56] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "List Evolution & Refinement"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Processo contínuo de refinamento, lista viva que evolui, aprendizado através da experiência

 

[44:58]"Now, this list, as I've described, there's the do nots, meaning we'll never touch people who have any of those traits. And there's the long list of dues, our perfect clients."

 

[45:08]"And as I've described, it constantly evolves. Uh, everything's always being tweaked. You know, there's always new bullet points that are being added into that."

 

[45:17]"Um, hopefully you've seen them going down the side here as well. But if you want the laundry list yourself of everything that I use inside of my agency, just go to the Facebook group, type in perfect client trades,"

 

[45:29] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Homework Assignment"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

 

VISUAL: Exercício prático: desenhar stick figure, duas colunas (do nots vs. perfect traits), aplicação personalizada

 

[45:31]"write down as a homework assignment in between this video and next one, a little stick figure, and then write do nots and what are my perfect client traits."

 

[45:41]"This will help you with every deal you take on. have your agency be smoother all around and just help you scale a little more efficiently."

 

[45:49]"I'll see you in the next video."

 

 

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO

 

NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:

3.5 anos de experiência operacional da agência mencionados

$10K/mês valor de serviço exemplo vs. $5K contraproposta inadequada

$10,000-$20,000/mês faixa confortável para clientes ideais

Dan Lok case: $25K base → $75K/mês ($150K/dia) → $150K/mês ($300K/dia)

Dan Lok crescimento: centenas de milhares/mês → milhões/mês

Projeto equity fracassado: 5,000+ entrevistas, 2.5 meses trabalho não pago, net worth de centenas de milhões do parceiro

Caso YouTube vs. Facebook: 9 meses operação inicial, 5-6 meses trabalho conjunto, 10% → 40% → 85% leads do Facebook

$50,000 ad spend → $60,000 retorno (insuficiente) vs. $50,000 → $250,000-$500,000 (ideal para escalar)

Proposta de escala: $100,000-$150,000/mês em ad spend

Lee Haight/Sky Diamonds: Ano 1 = $550,000, Anos 2-3 = $3M+ (após educação em marketing)

Crescimento exponencial desejado: $1M atual → $10M (1 ano) → $100M (2 anos)

40% redução fluxo sanguíneo cerebral por café

Ligações inapropriadas: 1:00 AM e 3:00 AM

Atraso de reunião: 3:00 PM agendado vs. 4:45-5:00 PM chegada

Ciclo de pagamento: 30 dias

$50,000 mastermind com 10 business owners (exemplo)

3 chargebacks recebidos no caso do cliente com dissonância cognitiva

 

ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:

Perfect Client Traits List: Lista evolutiva constantemente refinada mês a mês

Do Nots (Absolute Deal Breakers): 13 características que desqualificam clientes imediatamente

Perfect Client Traits (The DOs): 15 características que definem clientes ideais

Clear Process to Revenue (CPR): Metodologia para articular valor e processo de resultados

Reward-Based Incentives: Sistema de compensação baseado em performance e resultados excepcionais

Rainmaker vs. Handyman Philosophy: Foco em ações de alta receita vs. tarefas operacionais

Data-Driven Operations: Decisões baseadas em analytics, CRM, métricas verificáveis

Accountability Systems: Relatórios diários/semanais, calls estruturadas, transparência total

Time Respect Protocol: Agendamento obrigatório, avisos com 1-2 horas de antecedência

Standards Materialization Principle: Padrões determinam realidade de vida e negócios

Cognitive Dissonance Recognition: Identificação de clientes que operam em dados antigos vs. atuais

Mutual Investment Philosophy: Troca bilateral de valor, tempo, energia e atenção

Fire Fast Protocol: Demissão rápida de clientes problemáticos com reembolso

Equity Deal Requirements: Contrato assinado + service fee obrigatórios

Cultural Duplication System: Valores e crenças replicados em toda equipe de vendas e operações

 

ESTRUTURA:

Operational Pipeline Clarity: Salespeople → Staff → Accountants → Executives todos conhecem perfect client traits

Two-List System:

Lista de "Do Nots" (quem nunca aceitar)

Lista de "Perfect Client Traits" (quem buscar ativamente)

Evolution Framework: Listas refinadas mensalmente baseadas em experiência e observação de falhas de outros

No Time Bomb Policy: Zero tolerância para clientes questionáveis, apenas perfect fits

Long-Term Relationship Focus: Recurring revenue, respeito mútuo, parcerias duradouras

Educational Investment Chain: Cliente educa-se → Melhores conversas estratégicas → Melhores resultados → Mais crescimento

25 Cognitive Biases Application: Uso de vieses psicológicos (especialmente reward bias) em acordos

Staff Incentive Cascade: Reward-based incentives do cliente → agência → staff

Garbage Truck Filter: Eliminação de negatividade, reclamações, fofocas do ambiente

Sensory Data Protection: Controle rigoroso de informações que entram pelos sentidos da equipe

Asset-Ready Client Model: Clientes já posicionados com funnels, sistemas, infraestrutura

Highest Revenue-Driven Actions Only: Foco exclusivo em atividades que geram máximo ROI

Smart Action Velocity: Execução rápida sem resistência para maximizar ciclos mensais de 30 dias

Expert Positioning Authority: Confiança alta = velocidade alta = resultados rápidos

Good to Great Team Philosophy: Investimento em elevar equipes já funcionais para excelência

Homework Assignment Structure: Stick figure exercise com duas colunas para aplicação prática

 

DADOS FINANCEIROS:

Service fees não negociáveis para manter fairness e prioridade

Deep pockets requirement: conforto com $10K-$20K/mês em contractors

Modelo de escalabilidade: base fee + performance bonuses progressivos

ROI mínimo para escala: 5-10x retorno sobre ad spend

Equity deals: apenas com service fee + contrato assinado

Opportunity cost calculation: 2.5 meses trabalho não pago = perda significativa

Growth trajectory expectations: 10x em 1 ano, 100x em 2 anos

Ad spend scaling: $50K → $100K-$150K quando ROI comprova

Revenue transformation: $550K ano 1 → $3M+ anos 2-3 com educação

Chargeback protection: documentação, testimonials, estar no direito

 

DURAÇÃO: 45:49 minutos FORMATO: Vídeo educacional/treinamento de agência, apresentação direta para câmera com elementos visuais laterais
